HIGH COURT By sec. 60 of the Matrimonial Causes Act 1899 the Supreme G matrimonial causes jurisdiction has power, in a suit for judicial make such orders as it deems just for the custody maintenance ar of the children of the parties to the suit, either by interim orders b decree, "or by provisions in the final decree, or after the final decree, cation for that purpose.

Secs. 4 and 7 of the Deserted Wives a Act 1901, inter alia, give power to justices, upon complaint duly } they are satisfied that a child is left by its father without means of and that the father is able to contribute to its support, to make an the payment by the father of a certain sum weekly or othe purpose. Z In a suit by a wife the Supreme Court, by consent, made a de judicial separation, and gave the husband custody of the childre rights of access being reserved to the wife. The wife was at pregnant, but no order was asked for or made with regard tothe of the child about to be born.

After the birth of the child the proceedings against her husband before a magistrate, under the Wives and Children Act 1901, for maintenance of the child. Held, that the existence of the decree of judicial separation, regarded as the foundation for a plea of res judicata, erroneously the inferior Court, or as ousting the jurisdiction of the inferior Co a bar to the proceedings, inasmuch as the relief sought by the wife w which she might have obtained, and might still obtain, from the Court, and that a prohibition should go to restrain the inferior further proceeding in the matter. Ex parte Bindon, 4 S.R. (N.S.W.), 503, approved.

Sec. 16 of the Hvidence Act 1898 provides for the proof of certain other documents of a public nature by means of a copy certified under of the officer to whose custody the originals are entrusted ; and sec. bed inter alia, that evidence of the pendency or existence of pri Court may be given by the production of a certificate showing that the hand of the clerk of that Court or the officer having " ordinari custody of its records or proceedings. Held, that for the purpose of an application for a prohibition, a proceedings in the Police Court, certified by an officer describing relieving clerk of that Court, and as the person having the custo records, and signed by him as such relieving clerk, is sufficient proceedings to satisfy the requirements of sec. 23, but, semble, that more properly within the meaning of sec. 16, with the requirements of it strictly complies. ; Decision of the Supreme Court on this point, Hx parte Brown, (190 (N.S.W.), 691, reversed, and rule made absolute for a prohibition. — AppEaL from a decision of the Supreme Court of Ne Wales. OF AUSTRALIA. uit in the Supreme Court in its matrimonial causes jurisdiction judicial separation from her husband, the appellant, and by ent a decree was made for judicial separation, giving the sband the custody of the children of the marriage then living, d the wife certain rights of access to the children.

The wife dertook not to claim then or at any future time maintenance r herself. She was at the time pregnant, but no application s made in the suit for maintenance in respect of the child about born. After the birth of the child the wife took proceedings ea magistrate under the Deserted Wives and Children Act 1901, to compel the appellant to contribute towards the maintene of the child. the hearing in the Police Court objection was taken on of the appellant that the Court had no jurisdiction to ike an order by reason of the existence of the decree of the ipreme Court.

The objection was overruled, and an order made the payment by the appellant of a certain sum weekly towards support of the child. e appellant obtained a rule nisi for a prohibition from the eme Court on the ground that the magistrate had no jurision to make an order, and that the order was against natural e, but, on motion to have the rule made absolute, the Full discharged the rule on the ground that the copy of the dings before the Court was not properly certified in accorde with sec. 23 of the Evidence Act 1898: Ex parte Brown (1). om this decision the present appeal was brought by special Watt, (with him Wilson), for the appellant. The certificate s strictly in compliance with sec. 23. Sub-sec. (f) provides it may be under the hand of the clerk of the Court in which proceedings were taken, and in the present case it was signed the relieving clerk of Petty Sessions.

HIGH COURT in its matrimonial jurisdiction, and, thirdly, that the on against natural justice, inasmuch as the defendant was stil to proceedings at the suit of the complainant in the Supre in its matrimonial jurisdiction, and the complainant h abandoned her right to take such proceedings.

When the matter came before the Full Court, the obje taken that the copy of the depositions was not properly y The Court gave effect to that objection, and discharged th The difficulty arose in this way. The certificate was in thi — 'IH. H. ©. relieving clerk of Petty Sessions and Ch Magistrate having the custody of the records of proceedings the Stipendiary Magistrates at the Police Court Redfern do her certify," &c. The objection was taken that the certificate dir say that the officer certifying was the officer " ordinarily hi the custody of the records." The attention of the Supreme ( was unfortunately directed to the 23rd section of the Hvide (No. 11 of 1898), which provides:—* Where it is nece prove any of the following facts—(q) the conviction or acquit before or by any Court or Judge or Justice of any person ch with any offence; or (b) that any person was sentenced to a punishment or pecuniary fine by any Court or Judge or Just or (c) that any person was ordered by any Court or Ji Justice to pay any sum of money; or (d) the pendeney ore ence at any time before any Court, Judge, Justice, or other off person, of any suit, action, trial, proceeding, inquiry, ebarg matter, civil or criminal, evidence of such fact may be give the production of a certificate under the hand of clerk of such Court; or . . . (g) the officer having o the custody of the records, or documents or proceedings, or min of such Court or Judge or Justice; . . . ." Either of # persons therefore could give a certificate.

The objection v the officer did not certify as the officer ordinarily hw custody of the records. But he described himself as the re clerk of Petty Sessions, so that he did describe himself as the officers (f) who could give a certificate under that section. to the point that he was only "relieving" clerk, the Znterp li Act (No. 4 of 1897), sec. 31, provides that: " Where an Act OF AUSTRALIA. the contrary intention appears, the power may be exercised 1 the duty shall be performed by the holder for the time being he office." So that it did appear on the face of the certificate at he was one of the persons who could do what the Act required. was therefore nothing in the objection in point of fact. sec. 23 has, in truth, no reference to certifying copies of uments. It relates to the proof of certain facts, and the formal 'ificate is only as to the correctness of the matters alleged. on is to the fact, not the nature of the documents, that it This sort of certificate is a very old one.

The form is n in Dickinson's Sessions Guide, 6th ed., p. 249, in the followg words: "These are to certify that at the Assizes of general ivery of the gaol of our Lady the Queen holden at EAS) in due form of law indicted tried and convicted, for that and was thereupon ordered and adjudged by the "Jourt to be imprisoned mi " (Signed) C. D. clerk of the Assizes in the county of —-—or clerk of the Peace for the county of 'That is the certificate contemplated by sec. 23. The only m that has any real bearing on this case is sec. 16, which ides: "Whenever any book or other document is of such public nature as to be admissible in evidence on its mere roduction from the proper custody, and no Act exists which nders its contents provable by means of a copy, a copy thereof act therefrom shall be admissible in evidence, if it is—(a) proved to be an examined copy or extract; or (b) certified under » hand of the officer to whose custody the original is entrusted ." And the officer is required to furnish a certified copy n payment of a certain fee.

Sec. 15 provides that where by any het an original certificate, document, or proceeding, or a copy or act from any document, or an entry in any register is admisSible in evidence, a document purporting to be any one of these If therefore sec. 23 applied, this document was properly certified, VOL. Brown v. Brown. Griffith C.J, HIGH COURT Unfortunately the learned Judges of the Supreme contrary view, and allowed the objection to prevail, I think, therefore, that in that they were wrong, a rule ought not to have been discharged on the p objection, even if the rules as to the admissibility of evid applications for prohibitions were the same as on the tri action, which I must not be supposed to take for granted, It is unfortunate that we have not had the op learning the opinion of the Supreme Court on the main qu in the case.

We cannot, however, make the rule absolut 1 because it was discharged on an erroneous ground. If fo other reason it ought not to have been made absolute, we to affirm this judgment. It is necessary, therefore, to consider the real point case, that is, whether, after a decree has been made by the Su Court in its divorce jurisdiction, justices can, in effe that decree by an order made in proceedings before tl the Deserted Wives and Children Act 1901.

The objectio taken as if it were a matter of jurisdiction, but it may perh taken in another way. In the case of Hw parte Bindon wife had applied for an order for maintenance for herself u precisely similar circumstances, and the Court made a rule ab 0 for a prohibition restraining her from further proceeding matter. The grounds taken in that case were that the m had no jurisdiction, and that the matter was res judic therefore, that the wife, having elected to go into the Di Court and obtain a decree without any alimony, she go to the magistrate and ask for maintenance." He did the case on either of the grounds taken.

They were, both of them good grounds for a prohibition at common the second ground might perhaps be taken here under the Act. I propose however, to deal with the matter briefly matter of jurisdiction. First as to the effect ofthe deeree for judicial separation. — (1) 4 S.R. (N.S.

Sec. 60 provides: "(1) In any suit or other prong for obtaining a decree of judicial separation or of nullity solution of marriage the Court may—(a) make such orders as s just and proper with respect to the custody maintenance ad education of the children the marriage of whose parents is subject of such suit or other proceedings and (b) if it thinks 'it direct proper proceedings to be taken for placing such children 'inder the protection of the Supreme Court in its equitable 'urisdiction. (2) Such orders and directions may be made (a) time to time by interim orders before making the final oe: or (b) by provisions in the final decree or (c) from time to . ) oa the final decree upon application by petition for that e. "= In this case it appears that the Court has made a final decree. was made by consent of the parties, but that is not material. By ie decree it was provided that the husband should have the ly of the six children of the marriage then living. At that the wife was pregnant with another child, whose paternity he husband denied.

Clearly then the Court had jurisdiction to ¢ provision, by its decree or afterwards, for this child, but it not asked to do so. As was pointed out in Hx parte Bindon , it was open to the wife to make the application for alimony the child, but she chose not to do so. Further, it is open to the Court still to vary that decree on a proper application for that se. There is, therefore, still standing a judgment of the » which must be taken to have dealt with the whole matter h the magistrate has assumed to deal with.

In support of Proposition that a party who has the right to ask for certain telief is bound by silence in not asking for it, if authority is _ Wanted, I may refer to the case of Newington v. Levy (2). In that case a party had omitted in previous litigation to avail himof the opportunity of setting up a claim which he then sought set up.

It was held that by so doing he was estopped from relying upon it in a second action. Therefore the plea of res judicata would appear to be applicable. That being so, has the 'trate any right to interfere with and in effect vary an order (1) 4 8.R. (N.S.

A The principles on which inferior Courts will be restra dealing with matters outside their jurisdiction, are clea in Shortt on Mandamus and Prohibition, Part IV.

Cap. where the author says, quoting from Blackstone:— tin . . . . is a writ . . . . directed to 7tiam and parties of a suit in any inferior Court, commanding the cease from the prosecution thereof ; upon a suggestion that the cause originally, or some collateral matter arising th does not belong to that jurisdiction, but to the cognizance o other Court." Although I do not know of any instance e the same as this, it seems to me that when an inferior Court upon itself to vary an order of a superior Court it is a Court dealing with a matter that does not belong to its ju tion. It would be very singular if an inferior Court be allowed to vary an order of a superior Court, which the la Court had the power to vary. There are two passages in well-known judgment of Willes J.in Mayor of London v. which I will read.

In dealing with the classes of cases in there is an absence of jurisdiction in the inferior Court, prohibition is asked for upon that ground, he said:—*be there are exceptions which, from their very nature, must b raised in the Court below. These occur in cases where prohibition will not go for mere irregularity in the pro or even a wrong decision of the merits . . . but in whi instance, as refusal of a copy of the libel, in which case hibition is only quousque; or refusal of a valid plea to a su below, yet, if it be refused, then upon its validity and truth b established in the Court above, the prohibition is absolute: v. Steele (2).

In these cases there is entire jurisdiction ov subject matter." And then, after referring to another cl cases, where there is general jurisdiction over the subject (1) LR. 2 H.L., 239, at pp. 276, 27. (2) 12C.B.N.S., 383. OF AUSTRALIA. "But, whatever be the true conclusion upon this, the is unanswerable, that, if it appears judicially to the a ig Court that the special or inferior Court will not y the plea, the prohibition shall go without the idle cerey of tendering there a plea which is sure to be rejected." hat, either on the ground that the Court has undertaken to e with or vary the order of the superior Court, or on the nd that it will not take any notice of the fact that the Supreme has already dealt with the matter, there is a good ground a prohibition. isnot necessary for us to consider the question whether, under careumstances, there could be a leaving of the child within emeaning of the Deserted Wives and Children Act. That quescannot arise in these proceedings. r these reasons it appears to me that the jurisdiction of the was ousted, and I have no doubt that the learned Judges Barton J.

In this case the appellant was defendant to a nons issued on a complaint of the respondent, his wife, 'ing him with having left his daughter Mary Brown without ns of support, within the meaning of sec. 4 of the Deserted ives und Children Act (No. 17 of 1901). His defence in the ce Court was that the magistrate had no jurisdiction. The strate overruled this objection, and ordered the appellant to certain sum weekly for the maintenance of the child.

The nt obtained a rule nisi for a prohibition, but it was diswith costs by the Full Court on a preliminary objection, d against that order of the Supreme Court he now appeals to s Court. e grounds upon which the rule was granted were that the trate had no jurisdiction, inasmuch as the matter was excluwithin the jurisdiction of the Divorce Court, and that Court already made a decree of judicial separation between the rties, and that the order was against natural justice inasmuch the wife still had her right to take proceedings in the Divorce . defence is raised which the Court is incompetent to try, he H©. or A. 1905. ete Brown v, Brows, Griffith 0.3. H. ©. of A. Court for maintenance of the child in question.

HIGH COURT to deal first with the preliminary objection. The application was treated by the Full Court as o statutory prohibition, as appears by their reasons for dis the rule. The original depositions of the witnesses before the Court, but annexed to the applicant's affidavit copy of them, verified by the certificate of a Mr.

Con described himself in the body of the certificate as " Reliey of Petty Sessions, and Chamber Magistrate, having the e the records of proceedings before the Stipendiary Magis the Police Court, Redfern" (where the complaint was hi j the foot of the certificate Mr. Connell appended to his s the words " Relieving clerk of Petty Sessions and Chamber trate." On objection taken the Court held that the was not in accordance with the 23rd section of the Hvide (No. 11 of 1898), since it did not appear that the officer certitied was the officer "having ordinarily the custody records, or documents, or proceedings or minutes " of the Redl Court of Petty Sessions: sec. 23, sub-see. (1) (g). The C therefore declined to hear the matter as it then stood a charged the rule nisi.

The Interpretation Act (No. 4 of 1897) in its 31st section as follows: [His Honor read the section and proceeded:] It the relieving clerk of petty sessions was the holder for the ¢ being of the office of clerk of petty sessions, that the Bvidence by sec. 23, confers on him as the person having ordinarily, his tenure of office, the custody of the records &e. the po duty of giving such a certificate as was given in this case, that the certificate was sufficient in form and substance. does not remove the difficulty, for I cannot see that the pr case is affected by sec. 23 of the Zvidence Act atall. That ay to the proof of certain facts, such as the fact of conviction, ment, acquittal of persons charged with offences, and the fact certain proceedings have been taken. But the facts whi there contemplated seem to me to belong to a totally class of facts from the fact involved in the question, what evidence given in a particular proceeding.

There is paragraph of the several into which sec. 23 is divided » OF AUSTRALIA. the evidence which is the basis of the decision appealed m, or of anything more than the actual facts of the proceeds, as distinguished from the evidence taken in proceedings ; Tconfess Ido not know by which of the paragraphs contained it section it could be said that the evidence given at a Police was intended to be aimed at. jis not easy to suppose, however, that the legislature, in the Act, assuming that it was necessary to provide for ;, intended to leave wholly unprovided for the case of the that see. 16 of that Act may be looked on as the provision ng the ground. [His Honor read sec. 16 and proceeded :] no reason to doubt that the depositions are documents within section, and that a copy of them was admissible if " certified the hand of the officer to whose custody the original is ed." There is no doubt that the production of the original ocuments would have satisfied the Supreme Court, and it is quite to my mind that the relieving clerk of petty sessions was » person entrusted with the custody of all original documents. h as those which are the subject of these proceedings. That so, I think that sec. 16 was the section which could have properly applied to the case, as the depositions were an within that section. I see no reason, therefore, to doubt the depositions being documents within the meaning of , or that the findings of the magistrate were properly before e Court, and I am, therefore, of opinion that the reasons given the Court for the discharge of the rule nisi were not ent. the question whether the rule was rightly discharged does nd there.

It remains to be considered whether the grounds hich the rule had been granted, or any of them, are tenable, whether it appears on the face of the proceedings that the respondent had no lawful ground for her complaint. What is dispute in substance 7 Supreme Court in its matrimonial causes jurisdiction had, 24th March, 1904, in a suit in which the present respondent was s that it was ever intended to aim at facilitating the H. v. oF A. 1905. Barton J. petitioner, and the present appellant respondent, decreed a HIGH COURT separation between them, by consent. Among the terms o consent decree was this, that the present appellant should committed to him the custody of the children therein n being all the children who had up to that time been born | parties, and described in the decree as "the issue of the man The wife was to have certain access to the children, undertook not to claim then or at any future time du separation maintenance for herself. No maintenance course, mentioned as to the children whose custody was to the father.

The child in respeet of whom the main but the mother was enceinte of that child, and it was' about a fortnight after the decree. So that, when the de was made, the mother knew very well that there would be another child to be maintained, and it was open to to make an application in respect of it. And there can b doubt that the Court had jurisdiction to make an order that child. {t had assumed jurisdiction over the whole of matters in controversy between the parties.

Howey application was made for that purpose. By see. 60 of the Ma monial Causes Act 1899 it is enacted: [His Honor read section and proceeded :] So that the wife not only had the to make application at the time, but she could have done so any time after the decree. She has not done so, and the que is now whether the Court of Petty Sessions at Redfern had ju diction in respect of her application for maintenance at Police Court, or whether the Court had power to entertain or sho have entertained the application.

The case of Hx parte B (1), of which the Chief Justice has already stated the sub is a complete bar to the case set up under the Deserted Wives a Children Act, with respect to the position of a wife after a dee of judicial separation. If the application had been one of kind before us here, I feel sure that the Supreme Court w have given effect to this objection, and come to the same con as they did in the case of Hx parte Bindon. His Hon Chief Justice has also mentioned the case of Newington v. 4 (1) 4 S.R. (N.S.W.), 503.
LR. OF AUSTRALIA. her case which deserves mention on this part of the conersy. That is the case of Lord Tredegar v.

Windus (2), the ision in which is accurately described in the head note. "A intiff in equity filed his bill stating that a policy had become oc at law, and claiming to have it treated as valid in equity. bill dismissed the same plaintiff sued at law on the poliey. a that a bill would lie to restrain the action, and injunction ganted accordingly." The plaintiff having had the matter finally It with in equity could not re-open the matter there, nor could go afterwards to another Court with that object. So in + of the matters connected with the custody and maintenance the children in question, whether it is a question of jurisdiction not, and there may be some doubt as to that, I think that the istrate had no right to interfere, and, putting it on the lowest und, that the defence of the existence of this decree purporting deal with the whole of the matters in controversy between the usband and the wife, being really a defence of res judicata, was lear bar to the maintaining of the proceedings in the Police urt. On the facts as they r from the evidence it is abundantly clear that there could no such proceedings taken in the Police Court after the decree i judicial separation. The existence of that decree and of the with which it dealt were in point of fact the real ground up in the Police Court, and I cannot help thinking that that as a ground which ought to have prevented the magistrate om entertaining the application or, at any rate, from making order against the appellant, after proof of that bar, and that ie Supreme Court should for that reason have made the rule ute. HIGH COURT their conclusion would have been different. That, not a matter in respect of which, if it had stood alone, would have given special leave to appeal. The substantial tion arising in the case is whether the children of a m which has been under the consideration of the Divorce in respect of which a decree has been made, can be, y meaning of the Deserted Wives and Children Act 1901," or left without means of support," and whether a wife can an application for maintenance upon such desertion, ay Ido not think it necessary to deal with this matter from point of view of jurisdiction, as I quite concur in the obs of my learned brother the Chief Justice on that point.

The application was for a prohibition under see. 112 of Justices Act 1902, which is in reality a form of appeal, for ever it is seen that a mistake of law has been made, it is op the Supreme Court to grant a prohibition. On the hearingh ' the Police Court, Mr. Schrader, for the appellant, took the o tion that the magistrate had no jurisdiction to enterta : application, and cited Ex purte Bindon (1).

The consid of that objection necessitates an examination of the De Wives and Children Act 1901, and the Matrimonial Causes 1899. See. 4 of the former Act, so far as is material, that :—"In any case where—(a) any husband or father deserted his wife or child, or has left such wife or child means of support . . . . . any justice may, upon plaint on oath being made by such wife or by the moth ; such child, or by any reputable person on behalf of such mother, or child issue his summons requiring such husband, to appear before two justices to show cause wh Atal not support such wife or child," or, in certain cases justice may issue a warrant for the apprehension of the hust or father. By sec. 7 it is provided that :—" Upon the heari justices shall inquire into the matter of the complaint they are satisfied that the wife or child is in fact left means of support, and that the defendant is able to cont i the support of such wife or child" may make an order for thep ment of a certain sum by the defendant towards the mainten (1) 4 8.R. (N.S.
W.), 508. OF AUSTRALIA. his wife or child. It is clear that what the Act deals h is the enforcement of the duty that the law casts upon a her to maintain his children.

It assumes the existence of the ibility to support his children which the law ordinarily imposes pon the father. And where there is a marriage and children fthe marriage the liability follows as a matter of course. It to enforce that duty and liability that the jurisdiction is given. w, What is the position of the children of a marriage which s been the subject of a decision in the Divorce Court?

It does not by any means follow that the liability continues. That Court, pronouncing a decree of judicial separation, may by the terms the decree, alter entirely the obligations which flowed from the ndition of marriage and from paternity in the ordinary course of things, and substitute altogether different obligations. The in this case directed that the six children should live with the and be maintained by him.

The wife was at the time ceinte with a child which was born a few weeks after the paration decree. It is sworn by the applicant that during the divorce proceedings he denied the paternity of that child. Under circumstances, the Court, having dealt with the whole er and specifically with the whole of the children then born, st be taken to have had cognizance of every obligation then ng actually or potentially arising out of the marriage, and ohave made its decree with respect to all the questions that ght in such a proceeding be litigated between husband and vife.

It appears to me that while that order continues it is impossible that the provisions of the Deserted Wives and Ohildren ict could be applied. The defence raised, whether it is regarded an objection to the jurisdiction, or asa plea of res judicata, . Seems to me in substance to amount to this, that whenever the ody and maintenance of the children, and has made a decree, the only Court which can further deal with the rights under that narriage is the Divorce Court itself. hat defence is in my pinion a good one.
It was laid down in Ew purte Bindon (1), at any person who wishes to have such a decree varied must (1) 4 S.R. (N.S. W.), 503. H.

HIGH COURT go to the Divorce Court for a further order. The resp might have gone to that Court and asked to re-open the for the purpose of having a special adjudication as to the tenance of this child which has been born since the decree, that step has not been taken. She has chosen to mal attempt to invoke the assistance of the Deserted Wij Children Act, and in that she must fail.

There is one passage which I will read from the judgme Martin B. on this point, in Newington v. Levy (1), wh a direct bearing on this question He said :—* Again in mead v. Maple (2), my brother Willes says: 'I apprehe if the same matter or cause of action has already been fir adjudicated on between the parties by a Court of jurisdiction, the plaintiff has lost his right to put it in either before that or any other Court.

The conditions for exclusion of jurisdiction on the ground of res judicata are, same identical matter shall have come in question already Court of competent jurisdiction, that the matter shall have controverted, and that it shall have been decided.' If the p have had an opportunity of controverting it, that is the same. as if the matter had actually been controverted and decided." here it must be taken that the Judge decided the que the maintenance and custody of the whole of the children of marriage. But, assuming that he did not do so, there opportunity for the wife to have had an adjudication as maintenance of the child about to be born, and it must the be taken as against her, when she seeks to litigate that qu in another Court, that the Divorce Court has adjudicated For these reasons I am of opinion, on the main ground, prohibition will lie, and that the rule ought to have been m absolute. Appeal allowed.
